query: alternative splicing association with heart disease

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Gonadal Dysgenesis -- Biochemical
retrieved_docs.content: In Turner syndrome whether traditional 45, X, or one of the many mosaic forms, there is a decreased gene dosage since there is not a second X chromosome for genes to escape X inactivation from. The lack of these genes on a secondary X chromosome is believed to be a large part of what causes the stigmata of Turner syndrome [10] . The SHOX gene is one of the many genes implicated in Turner syndrome and codes for two homeodomain isoforms. One of these isoforms is seen in bone marrow fibroblasts and implicated to be a part of the cause of short stature in Turner syndrome [11] . The SHOX gene encodes a transcription factor and has a 61 amino acid motif allowing for DNA binding. Alternative splicing creates two separate products from the transcription of the SHOX gene. SHOXa is a longer protein with 292 amino acids and is present in skeletal muscle, the heart, the pancreas, and fibroblasts of the bone marrow. SHOXb is a shorter protein of 225 amino acids and shows increased levels in bone marrow fibroblasts [12] .

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Myotonic Dystrophy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: MBNL 1 protein is abundant skeletal muscle, whilst MBNL 2 in brain tissue. These proteins exhibit loss of function, as they are aggregated in the nucleus and unable to be utilized by the cell. Conversely, CUG-BP binds to cardiac troponin and exhibits gain of function via increased activation and phosphorylation. Elevated levels result in cardiac abnormalities, and abnormal insulin receptor function, elucidating the risk of diabetes. It also inhibits myoblast differentiation, resulting in loss of CIC-1 chloride channels through disruption of splicing. Defect in the splicing results in sustained muscle contraction with an inability to relax (myotonia). [4] [12] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Muscle Atrophy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: This explanation hypothesizes the damage motor neurons specifically to either neuronal sensitivity to spliceosome malfunction directly or indirectly through incorrectly spliced mRNA creating dysfunctional proteins key to neuronal function. [3] [15] Recent developments in the understanding of SMN protein regarding its ubiquitous presence and multiple functions in all eukaryotic cells have led to conclusions that SMA is not purely a motor neuron disorder with congenital heart disorder associations and sensory nerve pathology noted in type 1 SMA patients. retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Trinucleotide -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: There are a few different types of myotonic dystrophy, but we will again focus just on type 1 as an example. Myotonic dystrophy type 1 is an autosomal dominant disorder that involves a CTG repeat in the dystrophia myotonica protein kinase (DMPK) gene on chromosome 19. [11] Normal copy numbers range from 5 to 37, but those affected with the disease can have up to several thousand trinucleotide repeats. [11] These repeats cause abnormal splicing and subsequent RNA processing. [11] Patients will have cataracts, gonadal atrophy, frontal balding, and muscle weakness starting at the face, neck, and distal extremities, eventually involving the heart muscle and other internal organs in later stages of the disease. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia With Variant Genetics -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Of the many somatic gene mutations described, four alterations, NOTCH1, ATM, SF3B1, and TP53 mutations, are detected in over 5% of patients. Notch proteins control the development of hematopoietic cells by working as transmembrane receptors for the cell. Mutations in the coding and non-coding regions of these NOTCH1 proto-oncogenes can lead to worsening disease through splicing events and an overall increase in their activity. [32] ATM, as mentioned earlier, is a gene responsible for detecting damaged DNA and inducing cell apoptosis. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Danon Disease -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: There are three isoforms of LAMP2 protein, including LAMP-2A, LAMP-2B, and LAMP-2C. The LAMP-2 protein is in the lysosomal compartment. The cytoplasmic tail of the LAMP-2A isoform serves as a receptor for the uptake of proteins for lysosomal degradation. This process is known as autophagy. [6] The role of each specific LAMP-2 isoform is unclear, although it merits noting that LAMP-2A has ubiquitous expression while LAMP-2B is present in a higher proportion in cardiac tissue, skeletal muscle, and the brain. [7] However, the vast majority of LAMP2 genetic mutations affect all three protein isoforms, leading to Danon disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Scleroderma and Primary Myocardial Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Some of these non-MHC loci have also been implicated in other autoimmune diseases, such as systemic lupus erythematosus, suggesting that immune dysregulation is a very important factor in systemic sclerosis development. [12] Epigenetic factors, most notably involving post-translational modification processes, have been described in systemic sclerosis. These are thought to be triggered by environmental factors and commonly involve histone modification, DNA methylation, and changes in non-coding RNAs. The result is thought to be the promotion of profibrotic genes, suppression of antifibrotic molecules, and alteration of fibroblasts function. These changes were also found in some studies to be associated with vasculopathy, fibrosis, B-cell activation, and antibody production. [13] [14] [15] [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.content: Aberrant gene splicing can produce dysfunctional receptor isoforms. Some of these isoforms may form heterodimers with normal receptors, creating a dominant negative effect that impairs receptor function. Similarly, abnormal proteolytic cleavage of receptors can generate dysfunctional fragments that either lack signaling capability or exhibit altered functions.

[23] This prediction is about 92% accurate. DMD-associated DCM involve pathogenic variants affecting the muscle promoter and first exon.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Primary Protein Structure -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: A series of proteins called transcription factors aid RNA polymerases in their transcription function. These factors have several functions, mainly facilitating the binding of RNA polymerase II to the promoter sequence, forming the transcription initiation complex. The initiation complex recognizes the promoter region in the DNA as a signal for the transcription starting point. The consensus sequence TATTAA (called TATA box) is located 25–35 bases upstream of the initiation site, followed by a poly-G/C consensus sequence located 32–38 bases upstream. The transcribed RNA contains multiple segments called exons and introns. Exons are segments containing the encoding sequence for protein synthesis, while introns are intervening segments of nucleotides that are excised from the primary RNA transcript (pre-mRNA) in splicing. Splice sequences are found at the 5′ and 3′ tails of introns marked with the dinucleotide GU at its 5′ end and AG at the 3′ end. Alternative splicing is a mechanism used to create different isoforms of the same protein so that several protein isoforms can be created from a limited repertoire of genes. Alternative splicing is now known to deeply influence biological behavior (sex determination, cell differentiation, and programmed cell death). [3] The mechanism underlying alternative splicing is not fully understood yet, but histone modification signatures correlate with splice site switching via chromatin-binding proteins. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, TREX1 Mutations -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Previous studies have shown that TREX1 gene deletion in mice leads to inflammatory myocarditis and shortened lifespan due to an interferon-dependent autoimmune response. [7] Recessive missense mutations in TREX1 are mostly associated with AGS, whereas dominant frame-shift mutations are predominantly associated with RVCL. [4] The encephalopathy in AGS and the cardiomyopathy of TREX1 null mice are both autoinflammatory responses. Increased interferon-alpha (IFN-α) levels in AGS and SLE are similar to antiviral immune responses. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Thromboxane A2 -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: TxA2 is involved in multiple biological processes via its cell surface receptor, termed the TP. The TP is a G protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) expressed in various tissues and cells, including platelets, vasculature (smooth muscle and endothelial cells), lungs, kidneys, heart, thymus, and spleen. Two highly related isoforms of the TP (alpha and beta) arise through differential mRNA splicing, a characteristic exclusive to the human receptor. [9] In most tissues, both isoforms are expressed; however, only the TP-alpha form is in platelets. The TP not only undergoes activation by TxA2 but also isoprostanes, which are free radical-catalyzed peroxidation products of arachidonic acid without the direct action of COX enzymes.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Testing
retrieved_docs.content: RNA sequencing has the potential to be an efficient clinical diagnostic tool because of its ability to measure gene expression outside the physiological range, allele-specific expression, and defects in alternative splicing. [18] Sequencing RNA involves isolating the RNA, preparation of an RNA library, and utilization of next-generation sequencing technology. The transcriptome can then undergo analysis for any aberrant genes.

retrieved_docs.title: Cat Eye Syndrome (Schmid-Fraccaro Syndrome) -- Pathophysiology -- Cardiovascular Development
retrieved_docs.content: The endothelial-to-mesenchymal transition that drives endocardial cushion formation in the atrioventricular canal is highly sensitive to gene dosage within 22q11.1-q11.2. Elevated CECR1 ( ADA2 ) activity may disrupt local adenosine gradients essential for proper cushion maturation, while overexpression of genes adjacent to TBX1 interferes with neural crest–derived contributions to the cardiac outflow tract. Consequently, CES is associated with a high incidence of atrioventricular septal defects, tetralogy of Fallot, and interrupted aortic arch. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2136
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction can present in many distinct clinical and electrocardiographic forms, yet all of these entities share the common pathological involvement of the myocardium. The condition includes classic presentations such as Acute Q‑wave myocardial infarction and Acute non‑Q‑wave infarction – widespread, as well as various ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions (STEMI) that are defined by the specific coronary artery segment that is occluded. Examples of STEMI subtypes are Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the proximal, distal, marginal, septal, or posterior descending branches of the right coronary artery, and the proximal left anterior descending coronary artery. Additional classifications encompass Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction involving the left anterior descending coronary artery, Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, and Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the septal branch of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery. Non‑ST‑segment elevation presentations are also represented, such as Subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction. Postoperative settings can give rise to Postoperative acute myocardial infarction. Despite the diversity of terminology, anatomical location, and timing, each of these acute coronary syndromes is characterized by injury to the myokard (myocardial tissue). Recognizing that the myocardium is the universal site of injury across all these variants is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and the selection of appropriate therapeutic strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
retrieved_docs.content: tissue in the pathophysiology and evaluation of AMI. Morphologically, acute infarct tissue links postoperative acute myocardial infarction and anterior non‑ST‑segment‑elevation myocardial infarction, indicating shared pathological changes across these clinical presentations. Acute subendocardial infarction is classified under “other specified forms of acute ischemic heart disease,” connecting it to a broader group of acute ischemic cardiac conditions. Together, these relationships underscore that coronary artery obstruction at diverse arterial segments is the primary mechanism driving myocardial damage, and that the common histopathology and diagnostic classification of the various AMI phenotypes are essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 711
retrieved_docs.content: atherosclerosis and other heart disease, from other and ill‑defined heart disease, and from داء تصلب الشرايين القلبي الوعائي (cardiovascular atherosclerotic disease). The term other and ill‑defined heart disease is synonymous with تمدد الأوعية الدموية في القلب (vascular dilation in the heart), providing an alternative description for this ambiguous cardiac condition. Overall, these relationships illustrate a comprehensive network in which pericardial disease (مرض تأموري) encompasses structural, inflammatory, and procedural entities; coronary atherosclerotic pathology is intertwined with inflammatory morphologies and pericarditis; and specific clinical entities such as ischemic heart disease without angina are linked to broader atherosclerotic and ill‑defined cardiac diagnoses, all of which have implications for diagnosis, classification, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1103
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction encompasses a spectrum of disorders that all involve injury to the myocardium. Specific forms include Acute Q‑wave infarction – widespread, Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI), and Acute STEMI due to occlusion of distinct coronary branches such as the left coronary artery, the left anterior descending (LAD) artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the diagonal branch of the LAD, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and the septal branch of the LAD. Additional variants are Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to circumflex branch occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, and Acute widespread myocardial infarction. Despite the differing anatomic sites of arterial blockage and electrocardiographic patterns (Q‑wave versus ST‑segment elevation), each of these entities shares the common finding site of the myocardium, underscoring that the primary pathological substrate in all these conditions is myocardial tissue damage. Recognizing the specific coronary artery involved helps guide reperfusion strategies and risk stratification, while the universal involvement of the myocardium highlights the need for prompt diagnosis and treatment to preserve cardiac function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Other and ill-defined heart disease is synonymous with تمدد الأوعية الدموية في القلب, a term that describes dilation of the blood vessels within the heart. This equivalence indicates that the ambiguous classification of certain cardiac conditions corresponds to a pathological enlargement of the coronary or myocardial vasculature, which can affect cardiac function and warrants appropriate diagnostic evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2559
retrieved_docs.content: Acute Q wave infarction – widespread, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions caused by occlusion of the distal left anterior descending coronary artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the distal portion of the right coronary artery, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, or the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, as well as acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction involving the left anterior descending coronary artery and acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, all represent distinct presentations of an acute myocardial infarction that share a common pathological substrate in the myocardium. Acute non‑Q wave infarction, acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), acute widespread myocardial infarction, postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and subsequent anterior non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction are additional variants of acute myocardial infarction, each reflecting different electrocardiographic patterns, timing, or clinical contexts, yet each also localizes its injury to the myocardial tissue. Collectively, these entities illustrate how diverse coronary artery occlusions, ECG morphologies, and clinical settings converge on myocardial damage as the central finding site, underscoring the importance of recognizing the specific type of infarction for appropriate diagnosis, risk stratification, and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1870
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction, in its many clinical presentations, is consistently localized to the myocardium (myokard). Whether described as an acute Q‑wave infarction (including widespread forms), an acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction caused by occlusion of the left coronary artery, the right coronary artery, the proximal left anterior descending (LAD) artery, the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, the septal branch of the anterior descending branch, or any combination of these arterial territories, the pathological finding site remains the myocardial tissue. This also applies to acute non‑Q‑wave infarctions, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions (including widespread and subsequent anterior variants), and myocardial infarctions that occur during procedures. Recognizing that all these diverse infarct types share the myocardium as the affected organ underscores the central role of myocardial injury in the diagnosis, monitoring, and treatment of acute coronary syndromes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1551
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory morphology is identified as an associated morphological pattern of coronary atherosclerosis and other heart disease, indicating that inflammatory changes are commonly observed in the arterial lesions of this condition. Coronary atherosclerosis and other heart disease is categorized under the broader diagnostic group اضطراب قلبي, reflecting its classification as a type of cardiac disorder. Additionally, the condition coronary atherosclerosis and other heart disease is linked to the term خُنَاقٌ صَدْرِيّ, suggesting that this Arabic expression is used as a mapped synonym or descriptive label for the same disease entity. Together, these relationships highlight the inflammatory nature of the disease, its placement within the spectrum of cardiac disorders, and its cross‑linguistic terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 976
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to these STEMI morphologies, Acute infarct also includes Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction during procedure, Acute widespread myocardial infarction, Postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and Subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction. Recognizing that all of these distinct anatomic and clinical variants share the overarching concept of acute infarct helps clinicians accurately classify, risk‑stratify, and manage patients presenting with acute coronary syndromes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2472
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction encompasses a broad spectrum of disorders that all share the myocardium as the primary site of injury. The spectrum includes classic Q‑wave infarctions, ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions (STEMI) and non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions (NSTEMI), as well as non‑Q‑wave and widespread infarctions. These acute events can be further categorized by the specific coronary artery or branch that is occluded, such as the left anterior descending (LAD) artery (including its proximal segment, diagonal branch, and septal branch), the circumflex artery, the right coronary artery (RCA) and its posterior descending branch, and other branches supplying the myocardium. Additional clinical contexts include myocardial infarction that occurs during a procedure or in the postoperative period. Despite the diversity in electrocardiographic patterns, anatomic locations of occlusion, and clinical settings, each of these entities represents an acute injury to the myocardial tissue. Recognizing that all these variants involve the myocardium is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate therapeutic targeting of the responsible coronary vessel, and optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2197
retrieved_docs.content: Acute infarct is a cardiac pathology that can manifest with a wide range of morphologic patterns. It is associated with acute Q wave infarction – including the widespread form – and with acute Q wave myocardial infarction. Various forms of acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction are also linked to acute infarct, such as the general disorder and specific presentations caused by occlusion of distinct coronary segments: the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, the distal portion of the right coronary artery, the mid portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and right coronary artery occlusion overall. In addition, acute infarct encompasses acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, and acute myocardial infarction that occurs during a medical procedure. Non‑Q wave morphologies are included as well, with both acute non‑Q wave infarction and its widespread variant being associated with acute infarct. Furthermore, acute infarct is linked to acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction and to subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction, reflecting its relevance to both initial and later ischemic events. Together, these associations illustrate that acute infarct can present with diverse electrocardiographic and anatomic features, emphasizing the importance of recognizing its various morphologies for accurate diagnosis and management.
